title: Quadrilaterals & Polygons
blurb: Interior and exterior angle sums for any polygon; parallelograms and how to prove them; rectangles, rhombi, squares, trapezoids, and kites; and the quadrilateral family tree.
buildsOn: Triangle congruence and the 180° angle sum (Unit 4), parallel-line angle facts (Unit 3), and the midsegment idea (Unit 5) — every quadrilateral proof splits the shape into triangles.

lessons.title: Angles of Polygons
lessons.concreteComparison: A stop sign has eight sides, a honeycomb cell has six, home plate in baseball has five. However many sides a polygon has, the corners always add up to a total you can predict from the side count alone — no protractor needed.
lessons.objective: Find the interior angle sum (n − 2)·180° of any polygon, use the fact that the exterior angles of any convex polygon total 360°, and find each interior and exterior angle of a regular polygon.
concept: A **polygon** is a closed figure made of straight **sides**; an $n$-gon has $n$ sides and $n$ **vertices**. A polygon is **convex** if it has no dents, and **regular** if all its sides are equal AND all its angles are equal.
A **diagonal** joins two vertices that are not next to each other. Drawing all the diagonals from a single vertex slices the polygon into triangles.
From one vertex, an $n$-gon splits into exactly $n - 2$ triangles. Each triangle's angles total $180^\circ$ (Unit 4), so the **interior angle sum $= (n - 2)\cdot 180^\circ$**.

The **exterior angles** of any convex polygon — one turn at each vertex — always total $360^\circ$. For a **regular** $n$-gon, each exterior angle is $\tfrac{360^\circ}{n}$ and each interior angle is $\tfrac{(n-2)\cdot 180^\circ}{n}$.
lessons.keyIdea: An n-gon's interior angles sum to (n − 2)·180° because it splits into n − 2 triangles from one vertex. The exterior angles always total 360°, so a regular n-gon has 360/n at each exterior angle.

facts.title: Interior angle sum
facts.explanation: An n-gon splits into n − 2 triangles from one vertex.

facts.title: Exterior angle sum
facts.explanation: True for every convex polygon, no matter how many sides.

workedExamples.statement: Find the interior angle sum of a hexagon ($n = 6$).
Find each interior angle of a REGULAR octagon ($n = 8$).

commonMistakes.correction: It's $n - 2$ triangles, not $n$. Always subtract 2 first.

commonMistakes.text: Dividing the interior sum by $n$ for a polygon that isn't regular.
commonMistakes.correction: Only a **regular** polygon shares the sum evenly. An irregular polygon's angles can differ.

tryIt.statement: Interior angle sum of a pentagon ($n = 5$)?
Each exterior angle of a regular hexagon?

steps.title: Cut the polygon into triangles
beats: A **polygon** is a closed shape with straight **sides**; count them to name it ($5 =$ pentagon, $6 =$ hexagon…).
A **diagonal** connects two corners that aren't neighbours.
From one corner, draw every diagonal — the polygon falls into **triangles**.
Each triangle holds $180^\circ$, so we just count triangles.

steps.title: The interior angle sum
steps.teach: Change the number of sides. From one corner, the polygon splits into $n - 2$ triangles, so the angles total $(n - 2)\times 180^\circ$. A quadrilateral gives $2$ triangles = $360^\circ$; a pentagon gives $3$ = $540^\circ$.

steps.title: Counting the triangles
beats: Pick one vertex. You can draw a diagonal to every other vertex except itself and its two neighbours.
Those diagonals fan the polygon into **$n - 2$** triangles.
Each triangle contributes $180^\circ$ (Triangle Angle-Sum, Unit 4).
Total $= (n - 2)\times 180^\circ$.

steps.title: Interior angle sum
steps.intro: Subtract 2, then multiply by 180.

examples.prompt: Quadrilateral ($n = 4$). Interior sum?
Octagon ($n = 8$). Interior sum?

problems.prompt: Pentagon ($n = 5$). Interior sum?
Heptagon ($n = 7$). Interior sum?
A decagon has $n = 10$. Interior sum?

steps.title: Share the sum evenly
beats: A **regular** polygon has all angles equal.
So each interior angle $= \dfrac{(n-2)\times 180^\circ}{n}$.
Regular pentagon: $540 \div 5 = 108^\circ$ each.
Regular hexagon: $720 \div 6 = 120^\circ$ each.

steps.title: Each angle of a regular polygon
steps.intro: Find the sum, divide by n.

examples.prompt: Each interior angle of a regular hexagon ($n = 6$).
Each interior angle of a regular pentagon ($n = 5$).

steps.title: Regular interior angles
steps.intro: Sum ÷ n.

problems.prompt: Each interior angle of a regular octagon ($n = 8$)?
Each interior angle of a regular quadrilateral (a square)?
Each interior angle of an equilateral triangle ($n = 3$)?

steps.title: The exterior angles total 360°
steps.teach: Switch to exterior mode. At each corner, turn from one side to the next — that's an exterior angle. Walk all the way around and you've turned a full $360^\circ$, for ANY number of sides. So a regular $n$-gon has $\tfrac{360}{n}$ at each corner.

steps.title: Always a full turn
beats: Walk around the polygon; at each corner you turn by the **exterior angle**.
One lap = one full turn = $360^\circ$, no matter how many sides.
Regular $n$-gon: each exterior angle $= \dfrac{360^\circ}{n}$.
And interior $+$ exterior $= 180^\circ$ at every corner (a linear pair).

steps.title: Exterior angles
steps.intro: 360 ÷ n for a regular polygon.

examples.prompt: Each exterior angle of a regular hexagon ($n = 6$)?
A regular polygon has each exterior angle $= 40^\circ$. How many sides?

steps.title: Exterior angle work
steps.intro: Exterior sum is always 360.

problems.prompt: Each exterior angle of a regular pentagon?
A regular polygon's exterior angle is $30^\circ$. Number of sides?
The exterior angles of a $100$-sided convex polygon total —

steps.title: Why honeycombs are hexagons
steps.body: Bees tile their comb with regular hexagons because $120^\circ$ corners fit together perfectly — three hexagons meet at a point ($3 \times 120 = 360^\circ$) with no gaps and no overlap. Only triangles, squares, and hexagons tile the plane by themselves, and the hexagon uses the least wax for the most storage.

steps.title: Interior sum
steps.prompt: What is the sum of the interior angles of a nonagon ($n = 9$)?

steps.title: What you learned
points: Interior angle sum of an $n$-gon $= (n - 2)\times 180^\circ$ — it splits into $n - 2$ triangles.
Exterior angles of any convex polygon total **$360^\circ$**; each is $\dfrac{360^\circ}{n}$ when regular.

steps.title: You've finished "Angles of Polygons"
steps.body: You can find the angle total of any polygon and every angle of a regular one. Next: the most useful four-sided shape of all — the parallelogram — and the properties that make it special.

lessons.title: Parallelograms & Their Properties
lessons.concreteComparison: An extending mirror, a scissor lift, a pantograph arm — they all stay useful because each cell keeps its shape as a **parallelogram**: opposite sides stay parallel and equal as it folds. Those guarantees are the properties we prove here.
lessons.objective: Identify a parallelogram and use its four properties: opposite sides congruent, opposite angles congruent, consecutive angles supplementary, and diagonals that bisect each other.
concept: A **quadrilateral** is a four-sided polygon. A **parallelogram** is a quadrilateral with **both pairs of opposite sides parallel**.
**Opposite sides are congruent** and **opposite angles are congruent** — the pairs across from each other are equal.
**Consecutive angles are supplementary**: two angles that share a side sum to $180^\circ$ (they are same-side interior angles between the parallel sides, Unit 3).
The **diagonals bisect each other** — they cross at their shared midpoint, cutting each other into two equal halves.
lessons.keyIdea: A parallelogram has both pairs of opposite sides parallel. Then opposite sides and opposite angles are congruent, consecutive angles are supplementary, and the diagonals bisect each other.

facts.title: Opposite sides & angles
facts.explanation: Sides across from each other are equal; so are the angles.

facts.title: Diagonals
facts.explanation: They cross at their common midpoint.

workedExamples.statement: In parallelogram $ABCD$, side $AB = 9$. Find the opposite side $DC$.
One angle of a parallelogram is $70^\circ$. Find a consecutive angle.

commonMistakes.text: Thinking ALL four sides of a parallelogram are equal.
commonMistakes.correction: Only **opposite** sides must be equal. All four equal is the special case of a **rhombus** (a later lesson).
**Opposite** angles are congruent; **consecutive** angles are supplementary (sum $180^\circ$).

tryIt.statement: In a parallelogram, one angle is $115^\circ$. Its opposite angle is —
A parallelogram's diagonal is $16$; the diagonals bisect each other, so each half is —

steps.title: A quadrilateral with parallel sides
beats: A **quadrilateral** is any four-sided polygon.
A **parallelogram** has **both** pairs of opposite sides parallel.
The arrow marks show which sides are parallel — one pair single, one pair double.
From that single definition, four handy properties follow.

steps.title: The four properties, marked
steps.teach: Opposite sides are equal (matching tick marks) and opposite angles are equal (matching arcs). Tap to reveal the diagonals — they cross at their shared midpoint, cutting each other exactly in half.

steps.title: What's equal to what
beats: **Opposite sides** congruent: $AB = DC$ and $BC = AD$.
**Opposite angles** congruent: $\angle A = \angle C$ and $\angle B = \angle D$.
**Consecutive angles** supplementary: $\angle A + \angle B = 180^\circ$.
**Diagonals** bisect each other at the centre.

steps.title: Why opposite sides are congruent
steps.teach: Draw one diagonal and the parallelogram becomes two triangles. Alternate interior angles (Unit 3) plus ASA (Unit 4) finish it.

rows.reason: Alternate interior angles: $\overline{AB} \parallel \overline{DC}$ cut by transversal $\overline{AC}$ (Unit 3)
Alternate interior angles: $\overline{AD} \parallel \overline{BC}$ cut by transversal $\overline{AC}$
ASA (Unit 4): two angles and the included side $\overline{AC}$

steps.title: Opposite sides & angles
steps.intro: Across-from pairs are equal.

steps.title: Neighbours add to 180°
beats: Two angles sharing a side are **consecutive**.
They sit as same-side interior angles between parallel sides.
So they are **supplementary**: they sum to $180^\circ$.
Know one angle $\Rightarrow$ subtract from $180$ for its neighbour.

steps.title: Consecutive angles
steps.intro: Subtract from 180.

examples.prompt: One angle of a parallelogram is $70^\circ$. Find a consecutive angle.
One angle is $105^\circ$. Find a consecutive angle.

steps.title: Neighbour angles
steps.intro: Consecutive → sum to 180.

steps.title: The pantograph and the parallelogram
steps.body: A pantograph — the linkage that copies or enlarges drawings, and the arm that presses a train against overhead power lines — is built from parallelograms precisely because opposite sides stay parallel and equal as the arms swing. The geometry guarantees the tracing point mirrors the guiding point exactly.

points: A **parallelogram** has both pairs of opposite sides **parallel**.
**Opposite sides and opposite angles are congruent**; **consecutive angles are supplementary**.
The **diagonals bisect each other** — proven with alternate interior angles + ASA + CPCTC.

steps.title: You've finished "Parallelograms & Their Properties"
steps.body: You know what a parallelogram guarantees. Next: the reverse question — which clues PROVE a four-sided shape really is a parallelogram.

lessons.title: Proving a Quadrilateral Is a Parallelogram
lessons.concreteComparison: A carpenter nails together four boards and wants to be sure the frame is a true parallelogram before mounting it. There are five different checks — pass any ONE of them and the shape is guaranteed to be a parallelogram, no need to test the rest.
lessons.objective: Use the five conditions that prove a quadrilateral is a parallelogram — each the converse of a property from the previous lesson.
concept: A quadrilateral is a parallelogram if it passes **any one** of these tests (each is the converse of a Lesson-2 property):
**1.** Both pairs of opposite sides are **parallel** (the definition). **2.** Both pairs of opposite sides are **congruent**.
**3.** Both pairs of opposite angles are **congruent**. **4.** The **diagonals bisect each other**.
**5.** **One** pair of opposite sides is **both parallel and congruent** — this single pair is enough.
lessons.keyIdea: Five converse tests each prove a quadrilateral is a parallelogram: both pairs of opposite sides parallel, or both pairs congruent, or both pairs of opposite angles congruent, or diagonals bisecting each other, or one pair of sides both parallel and congruent.

facts.title: Five conditions
facts.explanation: Opposite sides ∥, or opposite sides ≅, or opposite angles ≅, or diagonals bisect, or one pair ∥ and ≅.

facts.title: The shortcut
facts.explanation: A single pair that is both parallel and equal proves it.

workedExamples.statement: A quadrilateral has both pairs of opposite sides congruent ($AB = DC$ and $BC = AD$). Is it a parallelogram?
workedExamples.solution: Yes — condition 2 (both pairs of opposite sides congruent) guarantees it.

workedExamples.statement: In quadrilateral $ABCD$, the diagonals bisect each other, cutting into halves of $5, 5$ and $8, 8$. Parallelogram?
workedExamples.solution: Yes — condition 4: the diagonals bisect each other ($5 = 5$, $8 = 8$).

commonMistakes.text: Thinking one pair of sides merely PARALLEL is enough.
commonMistakes.correction: One pair parallel alone gives only a **trapezoid**. You need that pair to also be **congruent** (condition 5), or a second condition.

commonMistakes.text: Requiring all five tests to pass.
commonMistakes.correction: **Any one** condition is sufficient — you never need to check more than one.

tryIt.statement: One pair of opposite sides is both parallel and congruent. Parallelogram?
Diagonals bisect each other with halves $x$ and $6$ equal. Then $x = $ —

steps.title: Turning properties into tests
beats: Lesson 2 said: *if* it's a parallelogram, *then* these properties hold.
Now run it backwards: *if* a property holds, is it *forced* to be a parallelogram?
For these five, yes — each **converse** is true.
Passing any **one** test guarantees a parallelogram.

steps.title: The clues that guarantee it
steps.teach: Any one of these marked patterns is enough: both pairs of opposite sides equal, both pairs of opposite angles equal, or the diagonals bisecting each other. Tap the diagonals — equal half-marks are a guarantee all by themselves.

steps.title: Any one is enough
beats: **1.** Both pairs of opposite sides **parallel**.
**2.** Both pairs of opposite sides **congruent**.
**3.** Both pairs of opposite angles **congruent**.
**4.** Diagonals **bisect each other**. **5.** One pair of sides **parallel AND congruent**.

steps.title: Why 'one pair parallel and congruent' works
steps.teach: Draw a diagonal, use the one parallel pair for alternate interior angles, then SAS and CPCTC show the OTHER pair of sides is parallel too.

rows.reason: Alternate interior angles: $\overline{AB} \parallel \overline{DC}$ cut by transversal $\overline{AC}$
CPCTC gives equal alternate interior angles, which prove the lines parallel (Unit 3 converse)
Both pairs of opposite sides are now parallel (definition)

steps.title: Is it guaranteed?
steps.intro: Match the clue to a condition.

examples.prompt: Both pairs of opposite sides congruent. Parallelogram?
Only one pair of opposite sides is parallel (nothing else). Parallelogram?
steps: One pair parallel alone is just a trapezoid.

steps.title: Guaranteed or not?
steps.intro: Any one of the five conditions is enough.

problems.prompt: Diagonals bisect each other. Parallelogram?
Both pairs of opposite angles congruent. Parallelogram?
One pair of sides parallel, a different pair congruent. Guaranteed?
options: No — that's not one of the five
problems.explanation: The parallel-and-congruent must be the SAME pair (condition 5). Mixed clues aren't enough.

steps.title: Make a condition hold
beats: Often you're given expressions and asked to **force** a parallelogram.
Set opposite sides equal (condition 2), or set diagonal halves equal (condition 4).
Solve the equation for the unknown.
Example: diagonal halves $2x$ and $10$ bisect $\Rightarrow 2x = 10 \Rightarrow x = 5$.

steps.title: Solve to guarantee it
steps.intro: Set the matching parts equal, then solve.

examples.prompt: Opposite sides $3x$ and $18$ must be equal (condition 2). Find $x$.
Diagonal halves $y + 2$ and $9$ must match (condition 4). Find $y$.

steps.title: Find the value
steps.intro: Equal parts → solve.

problems.prompt: Opposite sides $5x$ and $20$ equal. $x = $ —
Diagonal halves $2y$ and $14$ match. $y = $ —
Opposite angles $(4x)^\circ$ and $80^\circ$ equal. $x = $ —

steps.title: The surveyor's shortcut
steps.body: To confirm a plot of land is a true parallelogram, a surveyor doesn't measure every angle — they just check that the two diagonals cross at their shared midpoints. That single test (condition 4) is faster in the field and, by the converse we proved, completely settles it.

steps.title: Enough to guarantee?
steps.prompt: A quadrilateral has one pair of opposite sides that are BOTH parallel AND congruent. Is it necessarily a parallelogram?
steps.explanation: One pair of sides both parallel and congruent is sufficient (condition 5).

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Five conditions** each prove a parallelogram; passing **any one** is enough.
They are the **converses** of the Lesson-2 properties (opposite sides/angles, diagonals).
The neat shortcut: **one pair of sides both parallel and congruent**.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's decide what's guaranteed and solve for values that force a parallelogram.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Both pairs of opposite sides congruent. Parallelogram?
Only ONE pair of sides parallel, nothing else. Parallelogram?
problems.explanation: One parallel pair alone is a trapezoid.

problems.prompt: Diagonals bisect each other. Parallelogram?
Opposite sides $6x$ and $30$ equal. $x = $ —
Which is the SHORTCUT condition (a single pair suffices)?
problems.explanation: Condition 5 — parallel AND congruent, same pair.

problems.prompt: Diagonal halves $3y$ and $21$ match. $y = $ —

steps.title: You've finished "Proving a Quadrilateral Is a Parallelogram"
steps.body: You can now certify a parallelogram from a single clue. Next: the special parallelograms — rectangles, rhombi, and squares — and what makes each one extra.

lessons.title: Rectangles, Rhombi & Squares
lessons.concreteComparison: A door is a rectangle, a baseball diamond is a square, a diamond playing-card suit is a rhombus. All three are parallelograms with something extra — a right angle, equal sides, or both — and each extra adds a new guarantee about the diagonals.
lessons.objective: Use the special properties of rectangles (four right angles, congruent diagonals), rhombi (four congruent sides, perpendicular diagonals that bisect the angles), and squares (all of the above).
concept: A **rectangle** is a parallelogram with **four right angles**. Its extra diagonal property: the **diagonals are congruent** (equal length).
A **rhombus** is a parallelogram with **four congruent sides**. Its extra diagonal property: the **diagonals are perpendicular**, and each diagonal **bisects a pair of angles**.
A **square** is a parallelogram that is **both** a rectangle and a rhombus: four right angles AND four equal sides, so its diagonals are **congruent AND perpendicular**.
All three are parallelograms, so they **inherit every parallelogram property** (opposite sides/angles congruent, diagonals bisect each other) on top of their own.
lessons.keyIdea: Rectangle = parallelogram + 4 right angles (congruent diagonals). Rhombus = parallelogram + 4 equal sides (perpendicular diagonals that bisect the angles). Square = both at once.

facts.title: Rectangle
facts.explanation: Four right angles; the two diagonals are equal in length.

facts.title: Rhombus
facts.explanation: Four equal sides; diagonals meet at right angles and bisect the angles.

workedExamples.statement: A rectangle's diagonal $AC = 13$. Find the other diagonal $BD$.
workedExamples.solution: A rectangle's diagonals are congruent, so $BD = 13$.

workedExamples.statement: A rhombus has side length $7$. Find its perimeter.

commonMistakes.text: Thinking a rhombus has congruent diagonals like a rectangle.
commonMistakes.correction: A rhombus's diagonals are **perpendicular**, usually NOT equal. Equal diagonals belong to the **rectangle**; only the **square** has both.

commonMistakes.text: Believing every rectangle is a square.
commonMistakes.correction: A square is a **special** rectangle (also needs equal sides). Every square is a rectangle, but not every rectangle is a square.

tryIt.statement: A rhombus has side $9$. Its perimeter is —
A rectangle's diagonals are $2x$ and $16$, and must be equal. Find $x$.

steps.title: Parallelograms with a bonus
beats: Start from a parallelogram — it already has all four properties from Lesson 2.
Add **four right angles** → a **rectangle**.
Add **four equal sides** → a **rhombus**.
Add **both** → a **square**. Each bonus changes the diagonals.

steps.title: The rectangle: equal diagonals
steps.teach: Four right-angle corners. Tap the diagonals — they still bisect each other (it's a parallelogram) AND they come out equal in length, the rectangle's signature.

steps.title: The rhombus: perpendicular diagonals
steps.teach: Four equal sides (single ticks all round). Tap the diagonals — they cross at a right angle, the rhombus's signature, and each one bisects a pair of the rhombus's angles.

steps.title: Why a rectangle's diagonals are equal
steps.teach: A rectangle is a parallelogram, so opposite sides are congruent; the right angles plus SAS and CPCTC give equal diagonals.

config.given: Rectangle $ABCD$ (a parallelogram with four right angles); diagonals $\overline{AC}$ and $\overline{BD}$.

rows.reason: Opposite sides of a parallelogram are congruent (Lesson 2)

rows.statement: $\angle ABC$ and $\angle DCB$ are right angles, so $\angle ABC \cong \angle DCB$
rows.reason: All four angles of a rectangle are right angles; all right angles are congruent
CPCTC — the diagonals are corresponding parts

steps.title: Rectangle & rhombus facts
steps.intro: Equal diagonals (rectangle); equal sides (rhombus).

examples.prompt: A rectangle's diagonal is $20$. The other diagonal?
A rhombus has side $11$. Its perimeter?

steps.title: Special-parallelogram facts
steps.intro: Match the property to the shape.

problems.prompt: A rhombus of side $6$ has perimeter —
Which shape has PERPENDICULAR diagonals?
problems.explanation: Rhombus (and square). A plain rectangle does not.

problems.prompt: Which shape has CONGRUENT diagonals?

steps.title: Rectangle and rhombus at once
beats: A **square** is a parallelogram with four right angles **and** four equal sides.
So it is a rectangle (equal diagonals) **and** a rhombus (perpendicular diagonals).
Its diagonals are therefore **congruent AND perpendicular**, and they bisect each other.
Every square is a rectangle and a rhombus; the reverse isn't true.

steps.title: The square: both signatures
steps.teach: Equal sides, right-angle corners. Tap the diagonals — congruent AND perpendicular, bisecting each other. The square inherits every property of the parallelogram, rectangle, and rhombus.

steps.title: Square & solve
steps.intro: Use both signatures; set equal and solve.

examples.prompt: A square has side $5$. Perimeter?
A rectangle's diagonals are $3x$ and $18$ (equal). Find $x$.

steps.title: Squares & values
steps.intro: Congruent + perpendicular; equal sides.

problems.prompt: A square of side $8$ has perimeter —
A square's diagonals are —
problems.explanation: It's both a rectangle and a rhombus.

problems.prompt: Rectangle diagonals $4x$ and $24$ are equal. $x = $ —

steps.title: Why builders measure the diagonals
steps.body: To check a door frame or a deck is square, carpenters measure both diagonals: if the two diagonals are equal, the corners are true right angles (that's the rectangle property in reverse). It's faster and more reliable than trying to measure a $90^\circ$ corner directly.

steps.title: Whose diagonals?
steps.prompt: Which special parallelogram has diagonals that are perpendicular but NOT necessarily equal?
steps.explanation: A rhombus has perpendicular diagonals; they're equal only when it's also a square.

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Rhombus**: parallelogram + four equal sides → **perpendicular diagonals** that bisect the angles.
**Square**: both at once → diagonals **congruent AND perpendicular**; it inherits every property above it.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's use equal/perpendicular diagonals and equal sides across the three special shapes.

steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

Every square is also a —
problems.explanation: It has right angles (rectangle) and equal sides (rhombus).

steps.title: You've finished "Rectangles, Rhombi & Squares"
steps.body: You know the special parallelograms and their diagonals. Next: the quadrilaterals that are NOT parallelograms — trapezoids and kites.

lessons.title: Trapezoids & Kites
lessons.concreteComparison: A flower-pot silhouette and a bucket's cross-section are trapezoids — one pair of parallel sides, one not. A flying kite is, well, a kite — two pairs of equal sides meeting at the tips. Neither is a parallelogram, but each has its own tidy rules.
lessons.objective: Identify trapezoids (exactly one pair of parallel sides), isosceles trapezoids (congruent legs, congruent base angles and diagonals), the trapezoid midsegment (average of the bases), and kites (two pairs of consecutive congruent sides, perpendicular diagonals).
concept: A **trapezoid** has **exactly one pair of parallel sides**, called the **bases**; the other two sides are the **legs**.
An **isosceles trapezoid** has congruent legs. Then its **base angles are congruent** (each base has a matching pair) and its **diagonals are congruent**.
The **midsegment** of a trapezoid joins the midpoints of the two legs. Its length is the **average of the two bases**: $\tfrac{b_1 + b_2}{2}$.
A **kite** has **two pairs of consecutive congruent sides** (adjacent, not opposite). Its **diagonals are perpendicular**, and one diagonal bisects the other.
lessons.keyIdea: A trapezoid has exactly one pair of parallel sides; its midsegment is the average of the two bases. An isosceles trapezoid adds congruent legs, base angles, and diagonals. A kite has two pairs of consecutive equal sides and perpendicular diagonals.

facts.title: Trapezoid midsegment
facts.explanation: The average of the two parallel bases.

facts.title: Kite diagonals
facts.explanation: The diagonals of a kite meet at a right angle.

workedExamples.statement: A trapezoid has bases $8$ and $12$. Find its midsegment.
An isosceles trapezoid has a bottom base angle of $72^\circ$. Find the other bottom base angle.
workedExamples.solution: Base angles at the same base are congruent, so it is also $72^\circ$.

commonMistakes.text: Calling a parallelogram a trapezoid (or vice versa).
commonMistakes.correction: A trapezoid has **exactly one** pair of parallel sides. A parallelogram has **two** — so it is not a trapezoid under this definition.

commonMistakes.text: Thinking a kite's equal sides are opposite each other.
commonMistakes.correction: A kite's congruent sides are **consecutive** (they share a vertex). Opposite congruent sides would make a parallelogram.

tryIt.statement: A trapezoid has bases $6$ and $14$. Its midsegment is —

steps.title: Not every quadrilateral is a parallelogram
beats: A **trapezoid** has **exactly one** pair of parallel sides — the **bases**.
The two non-parallel sides are the **legs**.
A **kite** has two pairs of **consecutive** (side-by-side) equal sides.
Each shape has its own special rules, different from a parallelogram's.

steps.title: The trapezoid: one parallel pair
steps.teach: Just one pair of sides is parallel — the arrow marks show the two bases. The other two sides, the legs, are not parallel. This is what separates a trapezoid from a parallelogram.

steps.title: Average the two bases
beats: Join the **midpoints** of the two legs — that's the trapezoid's **midsegment**.
Its length is the **average** of the two bases.
Midsegment $= \dfrac{b_1 + b_2}{2}$.
Bases $8$ and $12$: midsegment $= \dfrac{8 + 12}{2} = 10$.

steps.title: Midsegment of a trapezoid
steps.intro: Add the bases, divide by 2.

examples.prompt: Bases $10$ and $16$. Midsegment?
Bases $5$ and $9$. Midsegment?

steps.title: Find the midsegment
steps.intro: Average of the bases.

problems.prompt: Bases $6$ and $14$. Midsegment $= $ —
Bases $12$ and $12$. Midsegment $= $ —
Midsegment $9$, one base $6$. The other base is —

steps.title: When the legs match
beats: An **isosceles trapezoid** has congruent **legs**.
Then the two **base angles** on each base are **congruent**.
The two **diagonals** are **congruent** as well.
The angles along one leg (top + bottom) are supplementary — they sum to $180^\circ$.

steps.title: The isosceles trapezoid
steps.teach: The legs carry matching ticks (congruent), the base angles carry matching arcs (congruent). Tap the diagonals — in an isosceles trapezoid they come out equal in length, just like a rectangle's.

steps.title: Isosceles trapezoid angles
steps.intro: Base angles equal; along a leg they sum to 180.

examples.prompt: A bottom base angle is $75^\circ$. The other bottom base angle?
steps: Base angles on the same base are congruent.

examples.prompt: A bottom base angle is $75^\circ$. The top angle on that same leg?
steps: Along a leg the angles are supplementary.

steps.title: Isosceles trapezoid facts
steps.intro: Congruent legs, base angles, diagonals.

problems.prompt: One bottom base angle is $68^\circ$; the other bottom base angle is —
In an isosceles trapezoid the diagonals are —
A bottom base angle is $80^\circ$; the top angle on the same leg is —

steps.title: The kite
steps.teach: Two pairs of consecutive equal sides — the top pair (single ticks) and the bottom pair (double ticks). Tap the diagonals: they cross at a right angle. A kite is not a parallelogram, but its diagonals are always perpendicular.

steps.title: Why a real kite flies
steps.body: A flying kite is built as a geometric kite because its perpendicular diagonals — the spars — split it into four right triangles that share the load evenly and keep the sail taut. The line attaches near where the diagonals cross, on the axis of symmetry, so the wind balances left and right.

steps.title: Average the bases
steps.prompt: A trapezoid has bases of length $7$ and $15$. How long is its midsegment?
steps.explanation: The midsegment is the average of the bases: $\tfrac{7 + 15}{2} = 11$.

steps.title: What you learned
points: A **trapezoid** has **exactly one** pair of parallel sides; its **midsegment** $= \dfrac{b_1 + b_2}{2}$.
An **isosceles trapezoid** has congruent **legs**, **base angles**, and **diagonals**.
A **kite** has two pairs of **consecutive** congruent sides and **perpendicular** diagonals.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's average bases, use base angles, and apply the kite's right-angle diagonals.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Trapezoid bases $9$ and $17$. Midsegment $= $ —
A trapezoid has how many pairs of parallel sides?
Midsegment $11$, one base $8$. The other base is —
A kite's diagonals are —
problems.explanation: They meet at a right angle.

problems.prompt: An isosceles trapezoid's diagonals are —
A kite's congruent sides are —
problems.explanation: Two pairs of adjacent equal sides.

steps.title: You've finished "Trapezoids & Kites"
steps.body: You've met every member of the quadrilateral family. Last stop: fitting them all into one family tree and naming any quadrilateral as precisely as possible.

lessons.title: The Quadrilateral Family
lessons.concreteComparison: Think of a family tree. A square is a kind of rectangle, which is a kind of parallelogram, which is a kind of quadrilateral. The further DOWN the tree you go, the more special the shape — and it inherits every property from every relative above it.
lessons.objective: Place any quadrilateral in the family hierarchy, name it as specifically as possible, and reason about which properties a shape must, might, or can never have.
concept: The **quadrilateral family**: every four-sided shape is a **quadrilateral**. Special branches are the **parallelogram**, the **trapezoid**, and the **kite**.
Down the **parallelogram** branch: a **rectangle** (right angles) and a **rhombus** (equal sides) are special parallelograms, and the **square** is both at once — the most special of all.
A shape has **every property of each type above it** in the tree. A square, for instance, has all the properties of a rhombus, a rectangle, a parallelogram, and a quadrilateral.

Name a shape by its **most specific** type, and use "always / sometimes / never" to reason: a square is *always* a rectangle, a rectangle is *sometimes* a square, a trapezoid is *never* a parallelogram.
lessons.keyIdea: Quadrilaterals form a hierarchy: square ⊂ rectangle & rhombus ⊂ parallelogram ⊂ quadrilateral, with trapezoids and kites on separate branches. A shape inherits every property of every type above it; name it by its most specific type.

facts.title: Inheritance
facts.explanation: A shape keeps every property of the types above it.

facts.title: Most specific name
facts.explanation: Call a square a square, not just a parallelogram.

workedExamples.statement: A quadrilateral has four right angles and four equal sides. Name it as specifically as possible.
workedExamples.solution: Four right angles + four equal sides = a **square** (the most specific type).

workedExamples.statement: True or false: every rhombus is a parallelogram.
workedExamples.solution: True — a rhombus is a parallelogram with four equal sides, so it is always a parallelogram.

commonMistakes.text: Naming a shape by a general type when a specific one fits.
commonMistakes.correction: Use the **most specific** name. A shape with four right angles and equal sides is a **square**, not just a 'parallelogram'.
A rectangle is **sometimes** a square (only when its sides are also equal). But a square is **always** a rectangle.

tryIt.statement: A parallelogram with perpendicular diagonals and four equal sides is a —
Every square is a rectangle: always, sometimes, or never?

steps.title: A family tree of shapes
beats: Every four-sided shape is a **quadrilateral** — the top of the tree.
Branches: **parallelogram**, **trapezoid**, **kite**.
Down the parallelogram branch: **rectangle**, **rhombus**, then **square**.
The lower you go, the more special — and the more properties you carry.

steps.title: The most special: the square
steps.teach: The square sits at the bottom of the parallelogram branch. Tap the diagonals — congruent (from being a rectangle) AND perpendicular (from being a rhombus), bisecting each other (from being a parallelogram). It inherits everything above it.

steps.title: Properties flow downhill
beats: A **square** is a rectangle, a rhombus, a parallelogram, and a quadrilateral all at once.
So it has **every** property those types have.
A **rectangle** is a parallelogram, so it has all parallelogram properties **plus** its own.
You never lose a property going down — you only gain more.

steps.title: Name it most specifically
steps.intro: Use the narrowest type that fits.

examples.prompt: Four right angles, four equal sides. Name it.
steps: Right angles → rectangle; equal sides → rhombus.

examples.prompt: A parallelogram with four right angles but sides $5$ and $8$. Name it.
steps: Sides not all equal → not a square. It's a **rectangle**.

steps.title: Most specific name
steps.intro: Narrowest type that fits.

problems.prompt: A parallelogram with four equal sides (no right angles) is a —
problems.explanation: Equal sides without right angles = rhombus.

problems.prompt: A quadrilateral with exactly one pair of parallel sides is a —
Four right angles AND four equal sides is a —

steps.title: Reasoning up and down the tree
beats: Going **down** is 'always': a square is **always** a rectangle.
Going **up** is 'sometimes': a rectangle is **sometimes** a square.
Across separate branches is 'never': a trapezoid is **never** a parallelogram.
Ask: is the second shape always higher in the same branch?

steps.title: Always, sometimes, or never?
steps.intro: Check the direction on the tree.

examples.prompt: A square is ___ a rhombus.
steps: Square is below rhombus on the branch.

examples.prompt: A parallelogram is ___ a rectangle.
steps: Rectangle is a special parallelogram (below it).

steps.title: Hierarchy reasoning
steps.intro: Down = always, up = sometimes, other branch = never.

problems.prompt: A square is ___ a parallelogram.
problems.explanation: A square is a special parallelogram — always.

problems.prompt: A rhombus is ___ a square.
problems.explanation: Only when it also has right angles.

problems.prompt: A kite is ___ a parallelogram.
problems.explanation: A general kite has no parallel sides, so it isn't a parallelogram.

steps.title: Why definitions are worth arguing about
steps.body: Mathematicians actually debate whether a trapezoid should be defined as 'exactly one' or 'at least one' pair of parallel sides. Under 'at least one', a parallelogram would count as a special trapezoid — reshaping the whole family tree. We use 'exactly one', which keeps the branches separate. Good definitions are choices, made to keep the logic tidy.

steps.title: Most specific
steps.prompt: A parallelogram has four right angles and four congruent sides. Its most specific name is —
steps.explanation: Four right angles AND four equal sides make it a square — the most specific type.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Quadrilaterals form a **hierarchy**: square ⊂ (rectangle & rhombus) ⊂ parallelogram ⊂ quadrilateral; trapezoids and kites branch off separately.
A shape **inherits every property** of the types above it — name it by its **most specific** type.
Reason with **always / sometimes / never** by direction on the tree.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's name shapes precisely and settle always/sometimes/never claims.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: A parallelogram with equal sides but no right angles is a —
problems.explanation: Equal sides, no right angles → rhombus.

problems.prompt: A square is ___ a rectangle.
problems.explanation: A square is a special rectangle.

problems.prompt: A rectangle is ___ a rhombus.
problems.explanation: They overlap only at the square.

problems.prompt: Exactly one pair of parallel sides means a —
A shape with four right angles and four equal sides is most specifically a —
A trapezoid is ___ a parallelogram.
problems.explanation: Exactly one parallel pair rules out a parallelogram.

steps.title: You've finished "The Quadrilateral Family"
steps.body: You can place and name any quadrilateral and reason across the whole family — the finale of Quadrilaterals & Polygons. Next unit: similarity, where shapes keep their form but change size.
